schools.summary: The Capernaum synagogue on another sabbath (Matt 12:9); the right hand 'withered' by injury or disease, not congenital (Robertson; Luke 6:6).
Bede: the withered hand = the human race dried up in good works, withered in Adam's plucking and restored by Christ's hands on the cross; Pseudo-Jerome applies it to the avaricious (Eph 4:28).
Theophylact: the sabbath miracle leads the Jews to the truth; the withered right hand = the soul that has ceased from right works, bidden to arise from sin.
Henry: the disabled man is the proper object of charity; the cure confirms that it is lawful to do good on the sabbath.

themes: the man with the withered hand
lawful to do good on the sabbath

schools.summary: 'They watched' (paretēroun, imperfect) = sly, malicious watching (Luke's middle voice accents their interest; Luke 14:1; 6:7); the same Pharisees as the cornfield, determined to accuse him via the sabbath.
Wesley: their pride, anger, and shame, often silenced, were now ripening into malice.
'Stand forth' = step into the open, a bold defiance of the spying enemies; Christ knew their thoughts (Luke 6:8).
Bede: the man set in the midst that the watchers might be moved to compassion and lay aside their malice.
Theophylact: 'arise' from sin and 'stand in the midst' — the withered soul summoned to be restored to virtue.

The Jewish maxim that not to do good is to do evil, not to save life is to kill (Barnes, Clarke; cf. Matt 25:45); the 'pitiless' alternative; their silence as confounded defeat (Lightfoot's Sanhedrin parallel).
Wesley: they held their peace, confounded though not convinced.

themes: to do good or evil on the sabbath
not to save is to kill

schools.summary: Mark's characteristic 'look'; anger at sin tempered by grief (a sign of moral health, the Man of Sorrows); pōrōsis = moral ossification, hearts callous/dead/blind by long resistance.
Calvin: a just and holy anger proving Christ's true human passions without excess; our passions are lawful but never sinless, needing the Spirit's correction.
Wesley: 'angry at the sin, grieved at the sinner' — the true standard of Christian anger.

debates.question: Was Christ's anger sinful, and are the passions in themselves sinful?

sides.position: A just, holy anger; passions are not sinful in themselves, only in excess
Our own anger, even when justified, is never wholly free from sin

schools.summary: The Pharisees plot at once with the Herodians (first named) to destroy him (Luke 6:11) — inverting 'save or kill'; hatred of his holiness and reproof the motive (Matt 22:16).
Bede: Herodians = Herod's servants, hostile as their lord was to John; Christ withdraws to the sea, the hour not yet come, an example to flee persecution.
Theophylact: the Herodians held the heresy that Herod was the Christ, the princes of Judah having failed.
Calvin: bitter rivals unite against Christ — ungodliness, however divided, joins with one consent against the truth of God.
Jesus withdraws to the sea knowing the plot (Matt 12:15), not from fear but because his time was not come; one of Mark's many recorded withdrawals; yet the Galilee multitude follows.
Clarke: when the wicked persecute Christ, the pious seek him; he who labours for God finds more than he loses.

The crowd from every quarter — Jerusalem, Idumaea (Edom, named only here; cf. Gen 25:30; Aretas, 2 Cor 11:32), beyond Jordan, Tyre and Sidon (cf. Matt 15:21); many likely Gentiles, the fame spread everywhere.
Wesley: the Idumaeans had professed the Jewish religion for over 150 years; those about Tyre and Sidon were Israelites of those coasts.

A 'boat' not 'ship' (a mistranslation), the disciples' fishing-boat kept in constant readiness to teach from and to move as Jesus did (cf. Matt 13:2); readied lest the eager crowd crush him, yet he stayed for them.
The healed fame draws a dangerous crush (epipiptein, falling upon him), eager not hostile, hoping for cure by touch; 'plagues' = scourges (mastigas, cf. Mark 5:29), a mistranslation that misleads.
Wesley: 'scourges' are afflictive disorders sent or permitted of God as a chastisement of sin.

themes: sickness driving men to the Physician

schools.summary: The demons repeatedly fall and cry 'Son of God' (= Messiah) in dread, not worship (imperfects of repeated action); Barnes makes their superior knowledge proof of real evil spirits (Mark 1:24; James 2:19); Ellicott: the cry could not be received as evidence (John 5:31-37).

themes: demons confess the Son of God

schools.summary: A vehement charge of silence on the demons (and the healed), as elsewhere in Mark (1:25, 34); the testimony came from an unworthy source and the time was not ripe — premature proclamation would have provoked the charge of imposture.
Wesley: it was not the time, nor were they fit preachers.
The mountain a place of all-night prayer before the choice (Luke 6:12); 'whom he would' = the free, effectual call (John 6:37; 17:6), the emphatic 'himself'.
Calvin: a perpetual rule to begin with prayer in choosing pastors; this is election to private discipleship with a view to the later apostleship, not yet the sending.
Bede: the call is of divine grace, not their zeal; the mount = lofty righteousness; the twelve answer the tribes, sent to the four quarters to baptize the nations.

'Ordained' = made/appointed (epoiēsen), 'whom he also named apostles' (sent ones, Luke 6:13); twelve for the tribes and as competent witnesses (Matt 19:28; Rev 21:14); the two purposes — to be with him (training) and to be sent to preach.
Clarke's threefold ministry — preach, heal souls, war on the devil; the power to heal and cast out as the equipment of the apostles, though the two are distinct.
Henry: that Christ could confer this power proves it was original to him — a Son in his own house, not a mere deputy.
Bede: the miracles bear witness to the greatness of their heavenly message.

themes: power to heal and cast out

schools.summary: Peter/Cephas = rock, for firmness; given at the first meeting (John 1:42), Mark recapitulating (Augustine).
Calvin: the name fits a steadiness lasting till death, not nullified by the denial; but the Papists wrongly infer the Church is founded on Peter.
Bede: the rock is Christ (1 Cor 10:4); Simon, believing on the Rock, is given the name Rock; Theophylact: counted worthy for present virtue despite his future fall.

debates.question: Does Peter's name mean the Church is founded on him?

sides.position: No — the rock is Christ; Peter's firmness is derived from believing on him
The name marks Peter's personal steadiness and primacy among the Twelve

schools.summary: Boanerges (Aramaic, 'sons of thunder'); Lightfoot/Clarke on the philology; Ellicott on the fiery zeal (Luke 9:54; Matt 20:21; James the proto-martyr, Acts 12:2) and the Apocalypse's thunderings.
Calvin: a powerful voice to thunder the gospel through the world — heard still from John's mouth.
Wesley: the warmth and impetuosity of their spirit and the power of their preaching.

themes: a voice to thunder the gospel

schools.summary: Identifications: Bartholomew = Nathanael (John 21:2), Matthew = 'gift of God', Thomas = Twin, Thaddaeus = Jude of James, Simon the 'Cananaean' = the Zealot (Ellicott).
Pseudo-Jerome's mystical etymologies of each name — Matthew 'given', Thomas 'abyss', Simon 'laying aside sorrow' / Zealot, etc.
Judas named last as the betrayer; Iscariot from his village (Kerioth) or the tribe of Issachar.
Calvin: Christ chose Judas knowingly to forestall the offence of apostate teachers — a devil among eleven angels whose fall confirmed rather than shook the Church, which must not rest on men.
Pseudo-Jerome: Judas the 'boaster,' Iscariot 'memory of death,' a type of proud heretics; Theophylact: the apostles counted worthy for present virtue, not repelled for future sin.
The crowd reassembles at Simon's house so densely they cannot eat or even teach (Mark's vivid touch); Barnes on religion consuming ordinary comforts yet usually consistent with diligent business.
Calvin: the verse is the hinge into the kinsmen's conspiracy — wonderful that Christ's own relatives, who should have aided him, judged him mad.

themes: the multitude's hunger vs the kin's blindness

schools.summary: 'His friends' = the kinsfolk (hoi par' autou; cf. v.31), come to seize him; 'beside himself' (exestē) a charge of derangement (cf. John 7:20; 10:20; 2 Cor 5:13); Lightfoot on the rabbinic idiom for passion's discomposure; Barnes: the world ever brands religious zeal as madness (Hos 9:7).
Calvin: even Christ's intimates missed the Spirit's power in him — faith comes not of flesh and blood but of heavenly grace.

themes: the kinsfolk seek to seize him

schools.summary: The Jerusalem scribes came to hinder Christ, their reputation swaying the people; unable to deny the exorcisms, they impute them to a league with Beelzebub ('lord of flies', prince of devils; Matt 12:24).
Henry: 'Satan goes out by consent' was their evasion; the learned scribes put the slander into the country folk's heads.
Wesley: how easily a learned man eludes the strongest proof of a work of God, never taking God into the question.
Pseudo-Chrysostom: a kingdom/house/city divided is desolated; if Satan expelled Satan his kingdom (which is holding men) would dissolve.
'In parables' = argument from analogy (Ellicott); crisp thrusts exposing the scribes' inconsistency; the verb for Satan's 'casting out' is that used of demons (Robertson).
Henry: Christ reasons freely that every mouth be stopped; Satan is no fool to act against his own interest.

themes: How can Satan cast out Satan?

schools.summary: Gill applies the divided kingdom to the world and to Satan's realm; Robertson on meristhē (parted into factions); the self-evident law that internal division destroys.
Pseudo-Chrysostom: a kingdom divided by civil war must be desolated — the principle Christ turns against the Beelzebub charge.
Gill: 'house' = any family; the law of division narrowed from kingdom to household, covering the whole range of human society (Robertson on meristhē).
Pseudo-Chrysostom: the desolation of division is exemplified in a house as in a kingdom — leaving Satan's charge no exception.
Gill: on the scribes' theory Satan must rise against himself; the law of division applied to the devil — 'he cannot stand, but hath an end' (telos echei, Robertson).
Pseudo-Chrysostom: to drive devils from men dissolves Satan's kingdom, which consists in holding men.
Henry: Satan is no fool to act against his own interest — the charge reduced to absurdity.

themes: if Satan be divided he is finished
bound from without, not divided within

schools.summary: The strong man = Satan, his goods = the possessed; Christ binds and plunders him (diarpasai, Matt 12:29; Isa 49:24) — the conqueror of Satan, not his ally.

Bede: Christ has bound the devil, snatched men from his snares into the Church, and distributed his house (the four quarters) to the apostles; Theophylact: he first vanquishes the demons to free their captives.

themes: souls freed as proof of conquest

schools.summary: The sweeping promise of forgiveness for all sins and blasphemies (Matt 12:31; Luke 12:10), even for revilers of Christ who repent (cf. Luke 23:34); set as a foil to the coming exception.
Bede: forgiveness is for the penitent — against Novatus (no pardon for the lapsed) and Origen (universal final pardon).
The unpardonable sin = deliberately ascribing the Spirit's works to the devil (committable today, Robertson); the better text reads 'eternal sin' (Ellicott; Matt 12:32; Heb 10:29).
Pseudo-Chrysostom: blasphemy against the Spirit is against God; Augustine: it is impenitence itself; Bede: those who deny the Spirit's deity from ignorance, not malice, are not guilty of it.
Henry: a sin against the last remedy — setting aside the Spirit's strongest conviction leaves nothing to bring to repentance.

debates.question: What makes the blasphemy against the Spirit unpardonable?

sides.position: Deliberate malice naming the Spirit's work the devil's, against conviction
Impenitence itself, the hardened heart that refuses the Forgiver

themes: ascribing the Spirit's work to the devil

schools.summary: Mark's added clause fixes the meaning: the warning was given because the scribes ascribed the Spirit's work to an unclean spirit; Ellicott notes their words tended to, without absolutely being, the completed sin.
Wesley: the verse defines the blasphemy plainly — ascribing to the devil the miracles Christ wrought by the Holy Ghost.

The kinsfolk of v.21 arrive; the brethren named first, unbelieving (John 7:5); the pathetic picture of mother and brothers crowded out; the 'brethren' are relations, not the Virgin's sons (Bede vs Helvidius).
Bede reads mother/brethren mystically as the synagogue and Jews standing without, guarding the letter, while the Gentiles enter; 'the word is within, and the light within.'
Theophylact: the verse does not deny his mother but honours her for her virtue.

The multitude seated in a circle (kuklō) with the disciples inner; the message whispered inward through the crowd; the well-attested 'and thy sisters' explaining 'sister' in v.35; the contrast of those within and the kin without.

themes: within by faith vs without by blood

schools.summary: Pseudo-Chrysostom and Bede: the question is no reproof of his mother but ranks loyalties — the soul above earthly kindred, the Father's mysteries above the mother's feelings; religion the bond of the heart, not the body.
Gill: the answer is addressed to the multitude who relayed the message that his mother and brethren sought him.

themes: not a rebuff but a ranking

schools.summary: Mark's vivid 'look round'; Christ points to the disciples and names them his family; Robertson's pathos for Mary sent home.
Chrysostom: honour kin by faith above kin by blood — one becomes 'mother' of Jesus by bringing him forth in the hearts of hearers.
Wesley (with Henry): the preference of true disciples to Mary after the flesh guards against the idolatrous honours later paid her.

themes: guard against excessive honour to Mary

schools.summary: Doing the will of God (believing and obeying Christ; cf. James 1:25; 1 John 2:17) is the bond of kinship — moral and spiritual, open to all; 'sister' embraces every believer (cf. v.32).
Pseudo-Jerome: we are his kin and joint-heirs if we do the Father's will — discerned not by sex but by deeds.
Henry: believers are dearer to Christ than mere relations in the flesh; a reason to honour those that fear the Lord.
